31.title: Futur I — ću, ćeš, će + infinitive
31.intro: The future tense is the easiest tense in Croatian: the short present of htjeti (ću, ćeš, će, ćemo, ćete, će) plus an infinitive you already know. The only trick is spelling — it depends on what comes first in the sentence.

items.hr: ću, ćeš, će, ćemo, ćete, će
items.en: the future helper — short present of htjeti
items.note: Unstressed clitics: they can never stand first in a neutral sentence.
Infinitive first: raditi drops its final -i → radit + ću, pronounced as one word but written as two.
Same rule for every -ti verb: gledati → gledat before the clitic.
Verbs in -ći keep the full infinitive: doći ću, ići ćemo.
If anything else starts the sentence, the clitic comes second and the infinitive keeps its full -ti form.
After a pronoun the clitic is in second position and the infinitive stays whole.

items.hr: neću, nećeš, neće, nećemo, nećete, neće
items.note: Stressed forms written as one word — they MAY start the sentence: Neću raditi u nedjelju.
Questions use the stressed forms + li: Hoćeš li...? Hoćete li...?

items.en: See you tomorrow (we will see each other).
items.note: With reflexive verbs, se joins the clitic cluster: vidjet ćemo se.

31.title: Future forms & radit ću spelling
31.intro: Drill the clitics, the negative forms and the word-order spelling rule.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct future helper for 'mi': Sutra ___ ići na more.
questions.explanation: mi → ćemo: Sutra ćemo ići na more.

questions.prompt: Complete: Radit ___ sutra. (I will work tomorrow)
questions.explanation: ja → ću. Infinitive first, clitic second: Radit ću sutra.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is spelled correctly?
questions.explanation: When the infinitive comes first, -ti verbs drop the final -i, and clitic and verb are written as two separate words: Radit ću.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct form: ___ ću u osam sati.
questions.explanation: Verbs in -ći keep the FULL infinitive before the clitic: Doći ću u osam sati.

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct form of učiti: Sutra ću ___ hrvatski.
questions.explanation: Because 'Sutra' starts the sentence, the clitic ću is already in second position — the infinitive keeps its full form: Sutra ću učiti.

questions.prompt: Put the sentence in order: 'Tomorrow I will buy a train ticket.'
questions.answer: Sutra ću kupiti kartu za vlak.
questions.explanation: The clitic ću stands in second position after Sutra; the infinitive stays whole: Sutra ću kupiti kartu za vlak.

questions.prompt: Say 'I will not work on Sunday': ___ raditi u nedjelju.
questions.explanation: The negative future uses the stressed one-word forms neću, nećeš... which may begin the sentence: Neću raditi u nedjelju.

questions.prompt: How do you ask a friend 'Will you come?'
questions.explanation: Questions use the stressed form + li: Hoćeš li doći? The unstressed ćeš can never start a question, and hoće li is 3rd person.

questions.prompt: Put the sentence in order: 'We will see each other next week.'
questions.explanation: Infinitive first (vidjeti → vidjet), then the clitic cluster ćemo se: Vidjet ćemo se sljedeći tjedan.

32.title: Verb aspect — the big idea
32.intro: Almost every Croatian verb comes in a pair: an imperfective verb for ongoing, repeated or unfinished actions, and a perfective verb for one whole completed event. English hides this in tenses ('was writing' vs 'wrote'); Croatian builds it into the verb itself.

items.en: to drink (process/habit) → to drink up (one completed act)
items.note: The prefix po- makes the verb perfective.

items.en: to be writing → to write (and finish)
items.note: Prefix na-.

items.en: to be reading → to read through (to the end)
items.note: Prefix pro-.

items.en: to be watching → to take a look
items.note: Prefixes very often make a verb perfective: po-, na-, pro-, u-...
učiti = the process, naučiti = the result.
Not all pairs use a prefix — some change the stem.
Habit or repeated action → imperfective piti.

items.en: I drank up my coffee and left.
items.note: One finished event → perfective popiti.

items.en: She was writing the letter all morning.
items.note: Duration, no statement about finishing → imperfective.

items.hr: Napisala je pismo i poslala ga.
items.en: She wrote the letter and sent it.
items.note: Completed, result matters → perfective.
Perfective verbs cannot describe what is happening right now — for 'I am writing' you must use the imperfective.

32.title: Ongoing or completed?
32.intro: Choose the right member of each aspect pair.

questions.prompt: Which aspect describes an ongoing, repeated or unfinished action?
questions.explanation: Imperfective = process, habit, duration. Perfective = one whole completed event.

questions.prompt: Svaki dan ___ novine.
questions.explanation: 'Svaki dan' signals a habit → imperfective čitati: Svaki dan čitam novine.

questions.prompt: Jučer sam ___ cijelu knjigu.
questions.explanation: One completed event ('the whole book, yesterday') → perfective pročitati in the perfekt: Jučer sam pročitao cijelu knjigu.

questions.prompt: Complete with the perfective partner of pisati (f. past): Ana je ___ pismo i poslala ga.
questions.explanation: The letter was finished and sent → perfective napisati: Ana je napisala pismo i poslala ga.

questions.prompt: Which sentence means 'I am writing an email' (right now)?
questions.explanation: Perfective verbs have no true present meaning — for an action happening now you need imperfective pisati: Pišem mail.

questions.prompt: Complete with the perfective partner of piti (m. past): ___ sam sok i otišao.
questions.explanation: One finished act of drinking → popiti: Popio sam sok i otišao.

questions.prompt: What is the perfective partner of kupovati?
questions.explanation: kupovati (buy regularly) / kupiti (buy once) — a pair that changes the stem instead of adding a prefix.

questions.prompt: Dugo sam učio hrvatski i napokon sam ___ padeže.
questions.explanation: napokon (finally) points to a completed result → perfective naučiti: napokon sam naučio padeže.

questions.prompt: Put the sentence in order: 'I drank up my coffee and went to work.'
questions.answer: Popio sam kavu i otišao na posao.
questions.explanation: Two completed events in a row → perfective verbs: Popio sam kavu i otišao na posao.

33.title: Genitive — čaša vode, nema kruha
33.intro: The genitive is the 'of' case: possession, quantities and many prepositions. Singular endings: feminine -a → -e, masculine adds -a, neuter -o/-e → -a. It is the most-used case after nominative and accusative.

items.note: voda → vode: feminine -a → -e.
kava → kave.
vino → vina: neuter -o → -a.
kruh → kruha: masculine adds -a.
After nema (there isn't) the noun is ALWAYS genitive.
The most famous nema-phrase of all.

items.en: little time, a lot of work
items.note: Quantity words (malo, puno, dosta, nekoliko) take the genitive.

items.hr: iz, od, do, kod, bez, blizu, pored
items.en: from, from, to, at (someone's), without, near, next to
items.note: These prepositions all take the genitive: iz Zagreba, bez šećera, kod prijatelja.
Possession = genitive: sestra → sestre.

33.title: Genitive transformations
33.intro: Turn nouns into the genitive after quantities, nema and prepositions.

questions.prompt: čaša ___ (a glass of water)
questions.explanation: voda → vode: feminine -a → -e in the genitive singular. čaša vode = a glass of water.

questions.prompt: Complete with the genitive of kava: šalica ___
questions.explanation: kava → kave: feminine nouns swap -a for -e. šalica kave = a cup of coffee.

questions.prompt: Complete with the genitive of kruh: Nema ___.
questions.explanation: After nema the noun must be genitive; masculine nouns add -a: Nema kruha.

questions.prompt: Which ending does a feminine -a noun take in the genitive singular?
questions.explanation: Feminine -a → -e (kava → kave). -u is the accusative, -i the dative/locative, -om the instrumental.

questions.prompt: Pijem kavu bez ___.
questions.explanation: bez (without) takes the genitive; masculine šećer adds -a: bez šećera.

questions.prompt: Complete with the genitive: Ja sam iz ___. (Zagreb)
questions.explanation: iz (from) takes the genitive: iz Zagreba, iz Splita, iz Amerike.

questions.prompt: U hladnjaku nema ___.
questions.explanation: nema + genitive; neuter mlijeko → mlijeka (-o → -a): U hladnjaku nema mlijeka.

questions.prompt: Put the sentence in order: 'One glass of water, please.'
questions.explanation: čašu is accusative (the thing you ask for), vode is genitive ('of water'): Molim vas jednu čašu vode.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'my sister's book'?
questions.explanation: Possession uses the genitive of the possessor: moja sestra → moje sestre. knjiga moje sestre = my sister's book.

34.title: Dative & locative — to someone, in somewhere
34.intro: The dative marks the person you give, say or send something TO. The locative uses the very same endings but ALWAYS follows a preposition (u, na, o) for static location or a topic. Learn them together — two cases for the price of one.

items.note: Masculine and neuter dative/locative singular: -u.

items.en: to a woman / to a sister
items.note: Feminine: -a → -i.

items.hr: ruka → ruci, knjiga → knjizi, noga → nozi
items.note: Sibilarization before -i: k → c, g → z (ruka → ruci, knjiga → knjizi).

items.en: I am giving my friend a book.
items.note: Recipient = dative (prijatelju), direct object = accusative (knjigu).

items.hr: pomagati, vjerovati, telefonirati, čestitati, zahvaliti + dativ
items.en: to help, believe, phone, congratulate, thank someone
items.note: These verbs govern the dative directly: Pomažem majci. Vjerujem ti. Telefoniram bratu.

items.hr: u gradu, na poslu, u školi
items.en: in the city, at work, at school
items.note: Locative = same endings, but always with a preposition: u/na for place.

items.hr: Pišem baki pismo. / Pričamo o baki.
items.en: I'm writing grandma a letter. / We're talking about grandma.
items.note: Dative = to/for someone (no preposition needed); locative = after u/na/o for place or topic.

34.title: Dative recipients & locative places
34.intro: Give things to people and say where things happen.

questions.prompt: Complete with the dative of prijatelj: Dajem ___ knjigu.
questions.explanation: The recipient takes the dative; masculine nouns add -u: Dajem prijatelju knjigu.

questions.prompt: Pomažem ___. (I help my mother)
questions.explanation: pomagati governs the dative; majka → majci (k → c before -i).

questions.prompt: Complete with the locative of knjiga: Sve piše u ___.
questions.explanation: u + locative; knjiga → knjizi with sibilarization g → z.

questions.prompt: Telefoniram ___. (I'm phoning my brother)
questions.explanation: telefonirati takes the dative: brat → bratu.

questions.prompt: Which case follows u/na when you say where something IS?
questions.explanation: Static location uses u/na + locative: u gradu, na poslu. (u/na + accusative would mean motion INTO/ONTO.)

questions.prompt: Complete with the locative: Živim u ___. (Zagreb)
questions.explanation: u + locative for where you live; masculine adds -u: Živim u Zagrebu.

questions.prompt: Put the sentence in order: 'I am writing grandma a long letter.'
questions.explanation: baki = dative recipient (baka → baki), dugo pismo = accusative direct object.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct sentence:
questions.explanation: o (about) takes the locative: posao → poslu. Razgovaramo o poslu = we're talking about work.

questions.prompt: Complete with the dative of sestra: Čestitam ___ rođendan!
questions.explanation: čestitati governs the dative: sestra → sestri. Čestitam sestri rođendan!

35.title: Describing people & places
35.intro: Rich description = adjectives + agreement. Looks use imati + accusative (Ima plave oči); personality and places use biti + nominative (Ona je duhovita, Grad je bučan).

items.note: f.: visoka / niska.
debeo is blunt — punašan (chubby) is kinder.
imati + accusative; the adjectives agree: duga smeđa kosa → dugu smeđu kosu.

items.hr: Ima plave oči i nosi naočale.
items.en: He/She has blue eyes and wears glasses.
items.note: oči is feminine plural → plave oči.
Feminine: ljubazna, duhovita, pametna, vesela, ozbiljna.
Also for places: lijep grad, ružna zgrada.

items.en: The city is big and noisy.
items.note: Places: biti + nominative adjectives.

items.hr: Moj stan je mali, ali svijetao.
items.en: My flat is small but bright.
items.note: svijetao (m.), svijetla (f.), svijetlo (n.).

items.en: The village is quiet and green.
items.note: Neuter agreement: -o endings.

35.title: Agreement in description
35.intro: Match adjectives to gender, number and case while describing people and places.

questions.prompt: Ana je ___.
questions.explanation: Ana is feminine singular → visoka.

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct form of kratak: Marko ima ___ kosu.
questions.explanation: kosa is feminine and here in the accusative after imati: kratka kosa → kratku kosu.

questions.prompt: What is the opposite of visok?
questions.explanation: visok = tall, nizak = short (of height).

questions.prompt: Ona ima ___ oči.
questions.explanation: oči is feminine plural → zelene oči (green eyes).

questions.prompt: Complete with the feminine form of duhovit: Moja sestra je jako ___.
questions.explanation: Feminine subject → duhovita: Moja sestra je jako duhovita.

questions.prompt: Put the sentence in order: 'My city is big and beautiful.'
questions.answer: Moj grad je velik i lijep.
questions.explanation: grad is masculine → velik i lijep, no ending changes in the nominative.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'He wears glasses'?
questions.explanation: naočale is a plural-only noun; the accusative after nositi is naočale: Nosi naočale.

questions.prompt: Complete: Stan je mali, ali ___. (bright, masculine)
questions.explanation: Masculine nominative: svijetao (note the -ije- spelling): Stan je mali, ali svijetao.

35.title: Kakva je nova kolegica?
35.intro: You are at a café with a friend who wants to hear all about your new colleague. Describe her looks and personality.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Čuo sam da imaš novu kolegicu. Kakva je?
lines.en: I heard you have a new colleague. What is she like?

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Zove se Ana. Jako je ljubazna i duhovita.
lines.en: Her name is Ana. She is very kind and funny.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: A kako izgleda?
lines.en: And what does she look like?

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Visoka je i ima dugu smeđu kosu i plave oči.
lines.en: She is tall and has long brown hair and blue eyes.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Nosi li naočale?
lines.en: Does she wear glasses?

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Ne, ne nosi. Uvijek nosi elegantnu odjeću.
lines.en: No, she doesn't. She always wears elegant clothes.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Odakle je?
lines.en: Where is she from?

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Iz Splita. Kaže da je Split ljepši od Zagreba!
lines.en: From Split. She says Split is more beautiful than Zagreb!

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ha! A kakav je njezin novi stan?
lines.en: Ha! And what is her new flat like?

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Mali je, ali svijetao i miran. Blizu je parka.
lines.en: It's small, but bright and quiet. It's near the park.

36.title: Dictation day — write what you hear
36.intro: Croatian spelling is phonetic: what you hear is exactly what you write. Dictation trains your ear and locks in the few genuinely tricky spots — č/ć, dž/đ and ije/je.

items.note: The golden rule of Croatian spelling — every sound has exactly one letter.
The dictation loop: play the sentence, say it aloud, then write it from memory.

items.hr: č vs ć: čaj, čovjek / kuća, noć
items.note: Both sound like 'ch' to beginners — learn which common words take which letter.
dž is written with two letters but is ONE sound.

items.hr: ije vs je: mlijeko, vrijeme, lijepo / djeca, čovjek, mjesto
items.en: milk, weather/time, beautiful / children, person, place
items.note: Long syllable = ije, short syllable = je. Listen for vowel length.

items.hr: Radit ću — two words, one sound
items.en: future forms are pronounced together but written separately
items.note: In dictation, remember the space: Radit ću, Gledat ćemo.
noć ends in ć — a classic dictation trap.

36.title: Spelling dictation drill
36.intro: Write the words exactly — diacritics count. This is your dictation without the audio: spell from meaning.

questions.prompt: Spell the Croatian word for 'milk'. (ije or je?)
questions.explanation: Long syllable → ije: mlijeko.

questions.prompt: Spell the Croatian word for 'children'. (ije or je?)
questions.explanation: Short syllable → je: djeca.

questions.prompt: Which spelling is correct for 'weather/time'?
questions.explanation: Long syllable → ije: vrijeme. (vreme is Serbian ekavian, not Croatian.)

questions.prompt: Which spelling is correct for 'house'?
questions.explanation: kuća takes ć. kuca (without the diacritic) means '(the dog) barks'!

questions.prompt: Spell the Croatian word for 'tea'. (č or ć?)
questions.explanation: čaj takes č — one of the most common č-words.

questions.prompt: You hear one word, 'raditću' — how do you WRITE it?
questions.explanation: The future is pronounced as one word but written as two: Radit ću.

questions.prompt: Spell 'Good night!' (two words, careful with the last letter)
questions.explanation: noć ends in ć: Laku noć!

questions.prompt: Spell the Croatian word for 'person/man'. (ije or je?)
questions.explanation: Short syllable → je, and it starts with č: čovjek.

questions.prompt: Rebuild the dictated sentence: 'The weather is nice today.'
questions.explanation: Both lijepo and vrijeme take ije: Danas je lijepo vrijeme.

37.title: Your 5-minute monologue toolkit
37.intro: Today you speak for five minutes mixing past, present and future. These frame phrases are the skeleton — hang this week's grammar on them.

items.en: First..., then..., after that..., in the end...
items.note: Sequence markers keep a long monologue organized.
Perfekt for everything in the past.
Present tense for now and for habits.

items.en: Tomorrow I will go to the seaside.
items.note: Futur I for plans — clitic in second position.

items.hr: prošli tjedan / ovaj tjedan / sljedeći tjedan
items.en: last week / this week / next week
items.note: Time anchors that instantly signal which tense is coming.

items.en: I think that... / I like... because...
items.note: Opinions and reasons stretch any topic by another minute.

items.hr: Kako se kaže...? / Kako da kažem...
items.en: How do you say...? / How shall I put it...
items.note: Repair phrases — keep talking instead of freezing.

37.title: Week 5 mixed review
37.intro: Futur, aspect, genitive, dative and locative — everything from this week in one drill.

questions.prompt: Sutra ___ učiti cijeli dan.
questions.explanation: Future plan → futur I: Sutra ću učiti cijeli dan.

questions.prompt: Complete with the genitive of voda: Popit ću čašu ___.
questions.explanation: čaša vode — 'of water' is genitive: voda → vode.

questions.prompt: Svako jutro ___ kavu.
questions.explanation: A habit ('every morning') → imperfective piti: Svako jutro pijem kavu.

questions.prompt: Complete with the dative of majka: Telefoniram ___.
questions.explanation: telefonirati + dative; majka → majci with sibilarization k → c.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is about the past?
questions.explanation: sam + participle radila = perfekt (past). ću + infinitive = future, radim = present.

questions.prompt: Put the sentence in order: 'Yesterday I studied, and tomorrow I will work.'
questions.answer: Jučer sam učio, a sutra ću raditi.
questions.explanation: Both clitics (sam, ću) sit in second position inside their own clause: Jučer sam učio, a sutra ću raditi.

questions.prompt: Živim ___ Splitu.
questions.explanation: Cities take u + locative: u Splitu, u Zagrebu.

questions.prompt: Say 'I will not work on Saturday': ___ raditi u subotu.
questions.explanation: Negative future = stressed one-word form: Neću raditi u subotu.

questions.prompt: Napisala je pismo. — What does the aspect tell us?
questions.explanation: Perfective napisati = one whole completed event — the letter got written.

37.title: Susjed na stubištu — small talk across three tenses
37.intro: Your neighbour catches you on the staircase. A perfect low-stakes chance to mix past, present and future — exactly what your monologue needs.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dobar dan, susjede! Kako ste?
lines.en: Good day, neighbour! How are you?

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Dobar dan! Dobro sam, hvala. Malo sam umoran jer sam cijeli tjedan puno učio.
lines.en: Good day! I'm well, thanks. A bit tired because I studied a lot all week.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: A što ste učili?
lines.en: And what were you studying?

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Učim hrvatski. Ovaj tjedan sam učio futur i padeže.
lines.en: I'm learning Croatian. This week I studied the future tense and cases.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Odlično govorite! Što ćete raditi za vikend?
lines.en: You speak excellently! What will you do at the weekend?

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Sutra ću se odmarati, a u nedjelju ću posjetiti prijatelje.
lines.en: Tomorrow I will rest, and on Sunday I will visit friends.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Lijepo! Mi ćemo ići na more ako bude lijepo vrijeme.
lines.en: Nice! We will go to the seaside if the weather is good.

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Sretan put! Vidjet ćemo se sljedeći tjedan.
lines.en: Have a good trip! We'll see each other next week.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hoćemo, naravno. Doviđenja!
lines.en: We will, of course. Goodbye!

38.title: Comparatives — stariji, ljepši, bolji
38.intro: Three regular patterns (-iji, -ji, -ši) plus four irregulars cover almost every comparison. The superlative is always naj- glued onto the comparative.

items.note: The default pattern: -iji; superlative = naj- + comparative.
Longer adjectives almost always take -iji.

items.hr: mlad → mlađi, jak → jači, skup → skuplji, tih → tiši
items.note: -ji fuses with the final consonant (iotation): d→đ, k→č, p→plj, h→š.

items.hr: lijep → ljepši, lak → lakši, mek → mekši
items.note: Exactly three adjectives take -ši — this is the whole list.

items.hr: dobar → bolji, loš → gori, velik → veći, mali → manji
items.note: The four irregulars — memorize them as words of their own.
od + genitive is the most common 'than'.
nego + nominative — same meaning, different construction.
Adverbs compare with the neuter form: mnogo → više, malo → manje, loše → gore.

items.hr: Ovo je najljepši grad na svijetu.
items.en: This is the most beautiful city in the world.
items.note: naj + ljepši, written as one word.

38.title: Bigger, better, the best
38.intro: Form comparatives and superlatives and compare with od/nego.

questions.prompt: What is the comparative of star?
questions.explanation: Default pattern -iji: star → stariji. najstariji is the superlative.

questions.prompt: Give the comparative of dobar (m. sg.):
questions.explanation: dobar is irregular: dobar → bolji → najbolji.

questions.prompt: Give the comparative of velik (m. sg.):
questions.explanation: velik is irregular: velik → veći → najveći.

questions.prompt: What is the comparative of mlad?
questions.explanation: -ji fuses with d → đ: mlad → mlađi.

questions.prompt: Kava je ___ od čaja.
questions.explanation: skup takes -ji with iotation p → plj: skuplji, feminine skuplja. Croatian never uses 'više' + adjective for comparison.

questions.prompt: What is the superlative of dobar (m. sg.)?
questions.explanation: Superlative = naj- + comparative: naj + bolji = najbolji, one word.

questions.prompt: Ivan je stariji ___ Marka.
questions.explanation: Marka is genitive, so the preposition must be od: stariji od Marka. (nego would take the nominative: nego Marko.)

questions.prompt: Put the sentence in order: 'My flat is smaller than yours.'
questions.answer: Moj stan je manji od tvoga.
questions.explanation: mali → manji (irregular); od + genitive tvoga.

questions.prompt: Danas se osjećam ___ nego jučer.
questions.explanation: 'I feel better' needs the ADVERB comparative (neuter form): dobro → bolje. bolji is the adjective.

39.title: Weather & seasons — Kakvo je vrijeme?
39.intro: Weather is the universal small-talk opener. Most phrases are impersonal ('It is sunny' = Sunčano je) or use padati/puhati ('rain falls', 'wind blows').

items.note: vrijeme means both 'weather' and 'time' — context decides.
Impersonal: adjective in neuter + je.
Literally 'rain/snow is falling' — kiša and snijeg are the subjects.
On the coast you'll hear the famous winds by name: bura and jugo.

items.hr: Vruće je. / Toplo je. / Hladno je.
items.en: It's hot. / It's warm. / It's cold.
items.note: The three temperature basics.
From magla (fog).
In a season: u proljeće, ljeti, u jesen, zimi.

items.hr: Ljeti je vruće, a zimi pada snijeg.
items.en: In summer it's hot, and in winter it snows.
items.note: ljeti and zimi are handy one-word adverbs.

items.hr: Kad je hladno, nosim jaknu i kapu.
items.en: When it's cold, I wear a jacket and a cap.
items.note: Weather links straight to clothing vocabulary.

39.title: Weather talk drill
39.intro: Seasons, weather phrases — and yesterday's comparatives recycled.

questions.prompt: How do you ask what the weather is like?
questions.explanation: kakvo asks 'what kind of' — Kakvo je vrijeme? (Koliko je sati? asks the TIME.)

questions.prompt: It's raining: Pada ___.
questions.explanation: Pada kiša — literally 'rain is falling'.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'It's sunny'?
questions.explanation: Impersonal weather: neuter adjective + je → Sunčano je.

questions.prompt: What is the Croatian word for 'winter'?
questions.explanation: zima = winter. Zimi = in winter.

questions.prompt: Which season comes after proljeće?
questions.explanation: proljeće (spring) → ljeto (summer) → jesen (autumn) → zima (winter).

questions.prompt: It's snowing: Pada ___. (careful: ije or je?)
questions.explanation: Long syllable → ije: snijeg. Pada snijeg = it's snowing.

questions.prompt: Put the sentence in order: 'In summer it's hot, and in winter it's cold.'
questions.answer: Ljeti je vruće, a zimi je hladno.
questions.explanation: ljeti/zimi are one-word season adverbs; a contrasts the two halves.

questions.prompt: Puše jak ___.
questions.explanation: puhati goes with wind: Puše jak vjetar = a strong wind is blowing.

questions.prompt: Danas je ___ nego jučer. (warmer)
questions.explanation: Impersonal weather comparison uses the neuter/adverb comparative: toplo → toplije. Danas je toplije nego jučer.

40.title: Body parts & Boli me...
40.intro: In Croatian your head hurts YOU: the body part is the subject and the person in pain is in the accusative — Boli me glava. Learn the ten core body parts and this pattern and you can explain any ache.

items.note: oči and uši are irregular plurals — among the oldest words in the language.
leđa is always plural: Bole me leđa.
Ten body parts cover nearly every complaint.
The body part is the SUBJECT; the person is accusative: boli + me/te/ga/je/nas/vas/ih.

items.hr: Bole me leđa. / Bole me zubi.
items.en: My back hurts. / My teeth hurt.
items.note: Plural body part → plural verb bole.

items.hr: Boli ga zub. / Boli je grlo.
items.en: His tooth hurts. / Her throat hurts.
items.note: ga = him, je = her — accusative clitics.

items.en: I have a fever. / I have a cold.
items.note: A woman says: Prehlađena sam.
From kašljati and kihati.

items.en: I need something for the pain.
items.note: protiv + genitive: protiv bolova, protiv kašlja.

40.title: Symptoms & the boli pattern
40.intro: Say what hurts — and make boli/bole agree.

questions.prompt: My head hurts: ___ me glava.
questions.explanation: Singular body part → boli: Boli me glava.

questions.prompt: ___ me leđa.
questions.explanation: leđa is plural, so the verb is plural too: Bole me leđa.

questions.prompt: What is the Croatian word for 'stomach'?
questions.explanation: trbuh = stomach/belly. Boli me trbuh = my stomach hurts.

questions.prompt: His tooth hurts: Boli ___ zub.
questions.explanation: The person in pain is an accusative clitic: ga = him. Boli ga zub.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'I have a fever'?
questions.explanation: imati + accusative: temperatura → temperaturu. Imam temperaturu.

questions.prompt: What is the Croatian word for 'throat'?
questions.explanation: grlo = throat. Boli me grlo = I have a sore throat.

questions.prompt: Put the sentence in order: 'My stomach has been hurting since yesterday.'
questions.explanation: boli + accusative me + subject trbuh; od jučer = since yesterday.

questions.prompt: Trebam nešto protiv ___.
questions.explanation: protiv takes the genitive: bolovi (pains) → protiv bolova.

questions.prompt: Someone says 'Prehlađena sam.' — who is speaking?
questions.explanation: The participle-like adjective agrees with the speaker: prehlađen (m.) / prehlađena (f.).

40.title: U ljekarni — Boli me glava
40.intro: You wake up with a headache and a sore throat, so you head to the pharmacy. Explain your symptoms and get something for the pain.

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Dobar dan. Trebam pomoć.
lines.en: Good day. I need help.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dobar dan! Izvolite, kako vam mogu pomoći?
lines.en: Good day! Go ahead, how can I help you?

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Boli me glava i grlo. Mislim da imam temperaturu.
lines.en: My head and throat hurt. I think I have a fever.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Od kada imate simptome?
lines.en: Since when have you had the symptoms?

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Od jučer. Loše sam spavao i jako kašljem.
lines.en: Since yesterday. I slept badly and I'm coughing a lot.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Jeste li alergični na neke lijekove?
lines.en: Are you allergic to any medicines?

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Ne, nisam. Trebam nešto protiv bolova.
lines.en: No, I'm not. I need something for the pain.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Uzmite ove tablete protiv bolova i sirup za kašalj. Jedna tableta svakih osam sati.
lines.en: Take these painkillers and cough syrup. One tablet every eight hours.

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Hvala. Koliko košta?
lines.en: Thank you. How much does it cost?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Deset eura. Ako ne bude bolje za tri dana, idite liječniku.
lines.en: Ten euros. If it isn't better in three days, go to the doctor.

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Hoću. Hvala vam puno. Doviđenja!
lines.en: I will. Thank you very much. Goodbye!

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Doviđenja i brzo ozdravite!
lines.en: Goodbye, and get well soon!
